questions.questionText: A 35-year-old man presents with well-defined erythematous plaques with thick silvery scales over extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp. Nail examination shows pitting and oil-drop sign. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Psoriasis (chronic immune-mediated disease with characteristic extensor distribution, silvery scales, and nail changes)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Psoriasis = well-defined, erythematous plaques with silvery micaceous scales on extensors + nail changes. Why correct: Classic presentation: symmetrical, extensor plaques + scalp involvement + nail changes (pitting — most common, oil-drop/salmon patch, onycholysis, subungual hyperkeratosis). HLA-Cw6 associated. Why A wrong: Eczema — ill-defined, itchy, flexural distribution, no silvery scales. Why C wrong: Tinea — annular with active scaly border + central clearing, KOH positive. Why D wrong: Pityriasis rosea — 'herald patch' followed by Christmas tree distribution, self-limiting. Clinical relevance: Types: plaque (most common), guttate (post-strep, drop-like), inverse (flexural), pustular, erythrodermic. Treatment ladder: topical (steroids, vitamin D analogs) → phototherapy → systemic (methotrexate, cyclosporine) → biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-17). Exam trap: Psoriasis is EXTENSOR; eczema is FLEXURAL. This distribution distinction is fundamental. Memory aid: 'Psoriasis = Plaques + Pitting + silvery Peeling + extensors.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Psoriasis.
questions.conceptTag: Psoriasis extensor silvery scales nail pitting

questions.optionB: Lichen planus (fine white reticular lines on the surface of violaceous polygonal papules — due to irregular thickening of granular layer)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Wickham's striae = fine white lines on lichen planus papules. Why correct: LP: 5 P's — Purple/violaceous, Polygonal, Planar (flat-topped), Pruritic Papules. Wickham's striae: fine white reticular network on papule surface (due to hypergranulosis). Koebner positive. Why A wrong: Psoriasis — Auspitz sign, silvery scales. Why C wrong: Pityriasis versicolor — hypo/hyperpigmented scaly patches (Malassezia furfur), 'spaghetti and meatballs' on KOH. Why D wrong: DH — grouped vesicles on extensors (celiac association). Clinical relevance: Oral LP: reticular white streaks on buccal mucosa (Wickham's pattern). Malignant potential of erosive oral LP. Histology: saw-tooth rete ridges, band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at DEJ, civatte bodies. Exam trap: Oral LP → premalignant. Cutaneous LP → NOT premalignant. Memory aid: 'LP = Lace-like striae of Wickham + Purple Polygonal Papules.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – LP.
